title: Suspensions are not the solution
subtitle: Suspending more children from school risks ignoring the reasons for their behaviour
paragraphs: I’ve just been talking to Radio Guernsey about suspension in schools. Suspension rates are rising, in both primary schools and secondary schools – and apparently there are some of the powers that be who think this is a Good Thing – because it shows that schools are Taking Control of behaviour.

I strongly disagree and here’s why. Suspension involves excluding a child temporarily, whether internally (in isolation) or externally (by sending them home). In some cases it may be necessary for everyone’s safety. However, it can also in some cases make the problem worse.

Suspending a child does indeed send them a firm message. It tells them that their behaviour is unacceptable – and often unfortunately what many of them learn is that they themselves are unacceptable too.
Suspension tells a child that they are not welcome at school. The idea is that this will lead to an improvement in their behaviour – but there really isn’t much evidence that it works like this.
That’s because children’s behaviour is never ‘just behaviour’. It’s communication. It’s feedback on how things are for them, both internally and externally.

For some, suspension becomes part of a cycle of alienation from education. It doesn’t break the cycle, it accelerates it. When they return to school they feel like they don’t belong there. They’ve been labelled as the bad kid, their behaviour gets worse – and so the next suspension comes faster. Soon they know the pattern and they may even deliberately break the rules so that the anticipation is over. It’s better to know.
If suspension worked as it is meant to there would be no repeat offenders. Behaviour would improve and that would be that. If a school is suspending more and more children, someone needs to ask why,
Suspension is an intervention that has the potential to cause harm. It doesn’t resolve the reasons why a child is behaving the way that they are. Our children need something better.
